+    const [activeSection, setActiveSection] = useState&lt;string&gt;(SECTIONS[0].id);

+    useEffect(() =&gt; {
+        const el = document.getElementById('history-article');
+        <span class="missing-if-branch" title="if path not taken" >I</span>if (!el) <span class="cstat-no" title="statement not covered" >return;</span>
+&nbsp;
+        const onScroll = () =&gt; {
+            type SectionId = (typeof SECTIONS)[number]['id'];
+            const sections = SECTIONS.map(({ id }) =&gt; ({ id, el: document.getElementById(id) })).filter(
+                (s): s is { id: SectionId; el: HTMLElement } =&gt; !!s.el
+            );
+            const scrollTop = window.scrollY + 120;
+&nbsp;
+            for (let i = sections.length - 1; i &gt;= 0; i--) {
+                const { id, el } = sections[i];
+                const top = el.getBoundingClientRect().top + window.scrollY;
+                <span class="missing-if-branch" title="else path not taken" >E</span>if (scrollTop &gt;= top) {
+                    setActiveSection(id);
+                    break;
+                }
+            }
+&nbsp;
+            setShowBackToTop(window.scrollY &gt; SCROLL_THRESHOLD);
+        };
+&nbsp;
+        window.addEventListener('scroll', onScroll, { passive: true });
+        onScroll();
+        return () =&gt; window.removeEventListener('scroll', onScroll);
+    }, []);

+<span class="cstat-no" title="statement not covered" >        window.print();</span>

How the Oehlers found their way to southern Minnesota, I do not know, but I hope to find out someday.

+                                &lt;/div&gt;
+                                &lt;p&gt;
+                                    Like most rural families in the early 1900s, the Oehlers relied heavily on their vegetable garden and spent many hours drying and canning fruits and vegetables. Harriet learned these essential farm food skills as a young girl.
+                                &lt;/p&gt;
+                                &lt;p&gt;
+                                    Eggs and chickens played a major role in their daily life. Harriet told stories of the family&amp;apos;s fear of &amp;ldquo;gypsies&amp;rdquo;ΓÇötraveling families who camped in a grove west of the house and were rumored to steal chickens and eggs at night. Minnie feared they might also steal her little girls, a fear that Harriet carried with her. Harriet also liked to tell, with some pride and a lot of humor, that her father drank raw eggs because he believed they settled his stomach and made him strong. She always ended the story with an emphatic, &amp;ldquo;AAACK!&amp;rdquo;
+                                &lt;/p&gt;
+                                &lt;p&gt;
+                                    Sundays were special at the Oehler home. Minnie usually took the girls to church, while Edward sometimes stayed home to prepare Sunday dinner. Afternoons were spent visiting family or friends. Minnie was also a fisherwoman. After Sunday dinner she would pack a picnic, hitch up the horse-drawn buggy, and take the girls to Lake Preston to fish and eat together.

Schafer means shepherd in German, and it seems likely the Schafers were shepherds and farmers for generations.

+                                    &amp;ldquo;Oliver told happy stories of growing up in a large farm family. When he was young, the fields were worked with horsesΓÇöPet and Patty were his favorites. East of the old farmhouse was an orchard of plums and apples. Oliver fondly remembered his sisters drying plums on screens laid out on the porch roof.&amp;rdquo;
+                                &lt;/div&gt;
+                                &lt;p&gt;
+                                    Large gardens, orchards, canning, drying, and baking were all part of daily life on the Schafer farm. Oliver grew up eating frequent breakfasts of cornmeal mush with sorghum syrup. Harriet would sometimes make it for him later in life, though not too oftenΓÇöshe did not care for it.

+/** Lifelike portrait avatars from randomuser.me (99 men + 99 women = 198). Used in avatar picker "Photos" set. */
+export const HERITAGE_AVATARS: string[] = (() =&gt; {
+    const base = 'https://randomuser.me/api/portraits';
+    const avatars: string[] = [];
+    for (let i = 1; i &lt;= 99; i++) {
+        avatars.push(`${base}/men/${i}.jpg`);
+        avatars.push(`${base}/women/${i}.jpg`);
+    }
+    return avatars;
+})();
+&nbsp;
+/** Illustrated avatars from DiceBear (avataaars). 48 options. Used in avatar picker "Illustrated" set. */
+export const ILLUSTRATED_AVATARS: string[] = (() =&gt; {
+    const base = 'https://api.dicebear.com/7.x/avataaars/svg';
+    return Array.from({ length: 48 }, (_, i) =&gt; `${base}?seed=${i + 1}`);
+})();
+&nbsp;
+/** All avatar sets for the picker: [label, urls]. */
+export const AVATAR_SETS: { label: string; urls: string[] }[] = [
+    { label: 'Photos', urls: HERITAGE_AVATARS },
+    { label: 'Illustrated', urls: ILLUSTRATED_AVATARS },
 ];
+&nbsp;
+/** Neutral silhouette used when a user has no avatar assigned (e.g. on sign-in). */
+export const PLACEHOLDER_AVATAR = `data:image/svg+xml,${encodeURIComponent(
+    '&lt;svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 100 100"&gt;&lt;circle cx="50" cy="50" r="50" fill="#d6d3d1"/&gt;&lt;circle cx="50" cy="38" r="18" fill="#a8a29e"/&gt;&lt;path d="M20 92c0-16 13-30 30-30s30 14 30 30z" fill="#a8a29e"/&gt;&lt;/svg&gt;'
+)}`;
+&nbsp;

       "Chill until set.",
       "Top with whipped cream. Family Food History Schafer / Oehler Family Food History By Julie Joy Schafer Johnson This family food history is drawn from the stories I remember being told by my parents, Oliver Schafer and Harriet (Oehler) Schafer, along with memories shared by our extended family. Our family has been involved in producing, preparing, and sharing food for centuries. The Oehler family originally came from Bavaria (southern Germany) to the United States in the mid-1800s. The name ΓÇ£OehlerΓÇ¥ means to produce or deal in oilΓÇölikely rendering oil from animals such as cattle, pigs, geese, and chickens, and selling it to others. How the Oehlers found their way to southern Minnesota, I do not yet know, but I hope to learn more someday. Gottfried Oehler and his wife Mary (who was from Switzerland) ran a general store in Wells, Minnesota in the 1870s. Their son Edward (born 1875\\) later farmed near Buffalo Lake with his second wife Minnie Willmert. They raised two daughters, Adelia and Harriet Wilma. Harriet often told how special it was as a child to receive an orange, an apple, peanuts, and hand-knit mittens at Christmas. Oyster stew was a cherished holiday meal. Like most rural families in the early 1900s, the Oehlers maintained large gardens and spent countless hours drying and canning fruits and vegetables. Eggs and chickens were central to both their diet and livelihood. Sundays were set apart. Minnie would take the girls to church, while Edward sometimes stayed home to cook Sunday dinner. Afternoons were for visiting or picnicsΓÇöoften at Lake Preston, where Minnie fished and packed picnic lunches. These outings helped shape HarrietΓÇÖs lifelong love of picnics and prepared her for years of cleaning and cooking fish alongside Oliver, an avid fisherman. The Schafer family immigrated from northern Germany in the mid-1800s, eventually settling near Sherburne, Minnesota. ΓÇ£SchaferΓÇ¥ means shepherd, reflecting generations of farming roots. John Daniel ΓÇ£JDΓÇ¥ Schafer and Dora Finke raised eleven children on the farm south of Buffalo Lake. Oliver, born in 1915, was the tenth. Oliver shared fond memories of farm lifeΓÇöhorses named Pet and Patty, walking to a one-room schoolhouse, orchards of apples and plums, and sisters drying fruit on screens on the porch roof. Cornmeal mush with sorghum syrup was a breakfast staple, a food he loved his entire life. Harriet and Oliver married in 1935 and weathered the Depression years together. They raised cattle, hogs, chickens, and crops, creating a true farm-to-table life. Harriet ran a serious egg business, delivering eggs to ΓÇ£The HatcheryΓÇ¥ on Main Street and keeping her own earnings. The kitchen, garden, chicken house, feedlot, and local lakes supplied nearly everything she cooked. Harriet was an exceptional farm cook. She made nearly everything from scratch, using ingredients fresh from her garden, her canner, or her freezer. The recipes in this book are not just instructionsΓÇöthey are a record of the work, resilience, and love that sustained our family across generations. Recipe Index (Alphabetical) B"

+function safeParseArray&lt;T&gt;(raw: string | null): T[] {
+    if (!raw) return [];
+    try {
+        const parsed = JSON.parse(raw);
+        return Array.isArray(parsed) ? parsed as T[] : <span class="branch-1 cbranch-no" title="branch not covered" >[];</span>
+    } catch {
+        return [];
+    }

+    return image.includes('pollinations.ai') || image.includes('images.unsplash.com') || (!image.startsWith('http://') &amp;&amp; !image.startsWith('https://'));
+}
+&nbsp;
+function normalizeRecipeImages(recipes: Recipe[]): Recipe[] {

+        if (!shouldUseCategoryFallbackImage(recipe.image)) return recipe;
+        return {
+            ...recipe,
+            image: getCategoryFallbackImage(recipe.category)
+        };
+    });
